results.possibleIdentity: Theodore Waddell (born 1930) is an American architect, industrial designer, and photographer who gained recognition in the late 1960s and 1970s for his experimental works blending art, architecture, and technology. After studying chemistry and business at Cornell University and architecture at the University of Pennsylvania, he moved to Italy in 1968, where he lived and worked for 15 years. He is particularly noted for his technical and 'participation' lamps designed for manufacturers like Zanotta and Cassina, such as the 'Il Globo' (1970) which used mercury to complete electrical circuits, and the '713/714' tensegrity tables for Cassina (1972). In 1969, the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) in New York held a solo exhibition of his work titled 'Function without Form: Two Models of an Undesignable City,' which featured complex models constructed from neon, pipes, and electronic components like pistons and spark plugs. This focus on the intersection of electronics and aesthetics aligns him with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement of that era. He eventually returned to the United States and settled in Santa Fe, New Mexico.

Theodore 'Ted' Waddell (born 1941) is a prominent American artist and rancher based in Montana, known for his modernist and abstract expressionist depictions of the American West. He was active in the late 1960s and 1970s, during which time he taught sculpture and design at the University of Montana (1968–1976). While he is best known for his 'heavy impasto' paintings of cattle and horses, his early career included the creation of minimalist polished steel sculptures. He studied at the Brooklyn Museum Art School and received his MFA from Wayne State University in 1968. His work is held in major collections including the Denver Art Museum and the Eiteljorg Museum. Although his work is primarily associated with Western modernism rather than the electronics-focused Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), his presence in national biennials and teaching role in design during the specified period makes him a notable historical figure of the same name.

results.possibleIdentity: Juan Downey (1940–1993) was a Chilean-born artist and a major pioneer in the fields of video art, interactive art, and electronic sculpture. Originally trained as an architect, he received his degree from the Pontificia Universidad Católica de Chile in 1961 and later studied printmaking in Paris. He moved to Washington, D.C., in 1965 and then to New York City in 1969, where he became a key figure in the experimental art scene. Downey is notably associated with the early movement of artists collaborating with engineers; he participated in the landmark 1968 exhibition 'Some More Beginnings,' which was organized by the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) group at the Brooklyn Museum. His early work featured interactive electronic sculptures and 'invisible architectures' that utilized sensors and feedback loops. In the 1970s, he gained international recognition for his groundbreaking video series 'Video Trans Americas' (1973–1976), which explored cultural identity and anthropology across the Americas using the then-new Sony Portapak system. He also created significant performance works like 'Plato Now' (1973), which incorporated biofeedback (alpha waves) to trigger philosophical texts. Downey served as an associate professor at the Pratt Institute from 1970 until his death.

results.possibleIdentity: Sidney J. Hurwitz (born 1932) is an American printmaker and artist who was prominently active during the late 1960s and 1970s. He is well known for his woodcuts, etchings, and aquatints that focus on industrial and architectural subjects, such as steel mills, factories, and urban structures. This focus on industrial themes aligns with the objectives of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), which aimed to foster collaborations between the arts and industry. Hurwitz was a Professor Emeritus at Boston University, where he taught for over 30 years, and was a resident artist at the Skowhegan School of Painting and Sculpture in 1968. His work is included in major collections such as the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA), the British Museum, and the Victoria and Albert Museum.

Jo Hurwitz was a central figure in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization during its peak years in the late 1960s and early 1970s. She served as the Executive Secretary and Administrative Director for E.A.T., working closely with founders Billy Klüver and Robert Rauschenberg. While primarily cited in historical archives for her administrative and public relations leadership—including managing membership and the E.A.T. newsletter 'Techne'—she was part of the creative circle of artists and engineers that defined the movement. Her name is frequently appearing in the institutional history of E.A.T. as a primary contact for artists and engineers.

Thomas Hurwitz (Tom Hurwitz) is an American filmmaker and cinematographer who was active in the late 1960s. In 1969, he and Peter Gessner screened their film 'Last Summer Won't Happen' at the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) as part of the Cineprobe series. Given the overlap between the New York independent film scene and the technological experiments of E.A.T. in the late 60s, Hurwitz is a notable historical figure associated with the era's experimental media projects. He later became a highly respected cinematographer, winning multiple Emmy Awards.
